draft.introduction: The Alvíssmál (“Sayings of All-Wise”) is an Eddic poem in which Thor detains the dwarf Alvíss until dawn by asking him to name things as they are called by gods, elves, dwarves, giants, and the dead. It is a mythological thesaurus in verse.

translation.text: Thor spoke: “Tell me, Alvíss—what the moon is called in each of the worlds.” Alvíss spoke: “Moon it is called by men, Fiery One by the gods, the Whirling Wheel in Hel, the Hastener by giants, the Bright One by dwarves, and by elves the Counter of Years.”
translation.original_text: Máni heitir með mǫnnum, en Mylinn með goðum, kalla Hverfanda Hvél helju í, Skiñunda jǫtnar, en Skín dvergar, kalla alfar Áratal.

translator_notes.note: The multiple names for the moon across mythological races reflect the Norse understanding of a multilayered cosmos where each order of beings perceives reality differently. This ontological pluralism—where the same object has different true names depending on who speaks—has no direct parallel in the biblical tradition.
